name: Hill of Uisneach
description: The Hill of Uisneach, located in County Westmeath, Ireland, holds immense significance in Irish mythology and history. It is considered the symbolic and geographical center of Ireland, representing the meeting point of the ancient provinces. According to tradition, Uisneach was where the five ancient provinces of Ireland (Connacht, Leinster, Munster, Ulster, and Mide) met, making it a place of great political and spiritual importance. The Bealtaine fire was traditionally lit here, marking the beginning of summer and a time of renewal. The Cat Stone (Aill na Mireann), a large limestone boulder, is a prominent feature, believed to mark the burial place of Ériu, a goddess associated with Ireland. Uisneach is associated with various deities and mythical figures, including the Dagda and Lugh, and is a site of numerous historical and archaeological remains, including ringforts, burial mounds, and standing stones. It represents a vital link to Ireland's pagan past and continues to be a place of pilgrimage and cultural significance.
subtitle: Geographical and Spiritual Center of Ireland

keyMyths.title: Bealtaine Fire
keyMyths.description: The Bealtaine fire was lit on the Hill of Uisneach, symbolizing the start of summer and a time of purification and renewal. This ritual marked the transition from the dark winter months to the light and abundance of summer.

keyMyths.title: Burial of Ériu
keyMyths.description: The Cat Stone (Aill na Mireann) is believed to mark the burial place of the goddess Ériu, the namesake of Ireland. This connection reinforces Uisneach's significance as a sacred and central place in Irish identity.

sources.author: Peter Harbison
sources.description: Provides archaeological context and historical analysis.
sources.text: Provides archaeological context and historical analysis.

sources.author: John Carey
sources.work: A Single Ray of the Sun: Religious Speculation in Early Ireland
sources.description: Explores the religious and mythological significance of Uisneach.
